sanskrit: किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रहस्तमिच्छामि त्वां द्रष्टुमहं तथैव । तेनैव रूपेण चतुर्भुजेनसहस्रबाहो भव विश्वमूर्ते ॥
hindiTranslation: मैं आपको वैसे ही किरीटधारी, गदाधारी और हाथमें चक्र लिये हुए देखना चाहता हूँ ।  इसलिये हे सहस्रबाहो  हे विश्वमूर्ते  आप उसी चतुर्भुजरूपसे हो जाइये ॥
englishTranslation: I want to see you again in your crowned form, with mace and discus in your hands. O Thousand-Armed One, O Universal Form, return to that four-armed shape.
essence: The infinite can overwhelm; devotion asks for a form the heart can bear.
speaker: Arjuna
simpleMeaning: Arjuna asks Krishna to return to the familiar four-armed form with crown, mace, and discus. The overwhelming cosmic form has moved him, but he longs for a shape he can meet with devotion.
simpleInsight: Awe can be too much to hold. The heart asks for a form it can love without fear.
detailedMeaning: On the Kurukshetra battlefield, Arjuna has just seen Krishna's vast cosmic form and feels shaken. He now asks Krishna to return to the four-armed form he knows, because the infinite vision is overwhelming and he can meet that gentler form with steadier devotion.
modernRelevance: You finally see the full truth of a situation, and it leaves you trembling. After the shock, you want something familiar enough to hold without breaking.
translationLiteral: किरीटिनम् (crowned) / गदिनम् (with mace) / चक्रहस्तम् (with discus in hand) / इच्छामि (I want) / त्वाम् (you) / द्रष्टुम् (to see) / अहम् (I) / तथा एव (just so, exactly) / तेन एव (by that very) / रूपेण (form) / चतुर्भुजेन (with four arms) / सहस्रबाहो (O thousand-armed one) / भव (be) / विश्वमूर्ते (O universal form)
insight: It teaches you that reverence can include a need for limits, and that love may ask for a form the mind can bear.
